---
title: "GitHub for analysts"
---

# GitHub for analysts

::: {.callout-warning}
## Placeholder chapter
Stub. To be drafted via the chapter-writing skill.
:::

## What this chapter will cover

GitHub is a website that hosts Git projects and adds a layer of collaboration tools on top. For an analyst it does three useful things: cloud backup, sharing, and (eventually) automated workflows.

- Creating a repository for your work.
- Pushing a local folder to a GitHub repo.
- Reading a project on GitHub: README, file tree, commit history, issues.
- The student benefits — free private repos and free Copilot Pro for verified students.
- A taste of GitHub Actions: how a `.yml` file in `.github/workflows/` can render a website on every push. (This very book deploys that way.)

## Why this matters for AI work

The capstone project at the end of the book is graded partly on reproducibility — can someone clone your repo and rerun your analysis? GitHub is how you make that question answerable.

## TODO for the author

- Walk through pushing a one-file repo from scratch.
- A short note on what *not* to commit: data files larger than a few MB, anything containing an API key, anything containing student personal data.
- A two-paragraph aside on `.gitignore` with a starter file the reader can copy.
